attributes.stem: A 28-year-old woman presents to the Labour and Delivery unit with spontaneous rupture of membranes at 39 weeks gestation. She is a primigravida with no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. She is motivated to have a vaginal delivery. On examination, she appears well. Her pulse rate is 80/min, blood pressure 120/70 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 36.8°C. Vaginal examination reveals a cervical dilatation of 3 cm. The midwife notices several painful vesicular lesions on the patient's labia and perineum. On further questioning, the patient reports a history of genital herpes diagnosed two years ago, with infrequent recurrences since then. She has not experienced any symptoms during this pregnancy until now.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate management plan?
options: Continue to monitor labour progress and reassess in four hours
Proceed with vaginal delivery and administer acyclovir to the neonate after birth
Proceed with vaginal delivery with maternal acyclovir administration
Use tocolysis to delay labour until lesions resolve
attributes.explanation:
This scenario presents a woman in early labour with active genital herpes lesions. The most appropriate management in this situation is to perform an emergency caesarean section. This approach minimizes the risk of neonatal herpes infection, which can have severe consequences including encephalitis, disseminated infection, and death.

Continuing to monitor labour progress and reassessing in four hours is not appropriate when active lesions are present at the onset of labour. The risk of transmission does not decrease with time, and delaying the decision for caesarean section may increase the risk if labour progresses rapidly.

Proceeding with vaginal delivery and administering acyclovir to the neonate after birth is also not appropriate when active lesions are present. While antiviral therapy for the neonate can be part of management if exposure has occurred, it does not prevent transmission during delivery. Prevention of transmission is key, which is best achieved through caesarean section in this scenario.

Proceeding with vaginal delivery with maternal acyclovir administration in the presence of active genital herpes lesions is not recommended due to the high risk of neonatal transmission. The risk of transmission to the neonate is highest when the mother has a first episode of genital herpes near term. However, recurrent episodes, as in this case, also pose a significant risk.

Using tocolysis to delay labour until lesions resolve is not a recommended approach. Tocolysis carries its own risks and is typically reserved for specific indications such as preterm labour. Moreover, herpes lesions may take days to weeks to resolve completely, which is not a feasible timeframe in this situation.

It's important to note that in women with a history of genital herpes but no active lesions at the onset of labour, vaginal delivery can be considered. Suppressive antiviral therapy in the last few weeks of pregnancy can reduce the risk of recurrence at term. However, when active lesions are present, as in this case, caesarean section is the safest option for preventing neonatal herpes infection.
learningPoints: Active genital herpes lesions at the onset of labour are an indication for caesarean section to prevent neonatal herpes infection.
Vaginal delivery can be considered in women with a history of genital herpes but no active lesions at the onset of labour.
Suppressive antiviral therapy in late pregnancy can reduce the risk of herpes recurrence at term.
references: Australasian Society for Infectious Diseases (ASID). Management of perinatal infections. Melbourne: The Royal Australian and New Zealand College of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists (RANZCOG); 2014. Available from: https://ranzcog.edu.au/wp-content/uploads/2022/05/ASID-Management-of-Perinatal-Infections.pdf
